target.name: Skeleton Technologies
target.founder: Oliver Ahlberg, Taavi Madiberk, Jaan Leis, Anti Perkson

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Skeleton Technologies is a Series E climate and energy company that has raised €392M to date. The valuation analysis is constrained by a limited comparable universe: while nine private peers operate in Climate & Energy, the peer-median method is inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage comparables (zero identified at Series E, two at adjacent stages, zero within similar valuation tiers). The broader comp set comprises one public company (LNZA) and eight private peers including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen, but these are dispersed across stage and sector subsegment, limiting their direct relevance.

Given the methodological constraints, triangulation via peer-median benchmarking cannot be reliably executed. The sparse and heterogeneous comparable set—particularly the absence of three or more same-stage, similar-tier Climate & Energy peers—precludes the application of conventional relative-value frameworks. While the identified private peers and single public comparable provide directional context, their valuation multiples (revenue, capital deployed, or other metrics) are too distant in stage or business model to anchor a precise valuation range.

The analysis indicates that a credible valuation range cannot be derived from the available data without introducing material estimation error. Further valuation work would require either: (1) expansion of the peer set to include early-stage growth or crossover-stage comparables with disclosed metrics; or (2) application of alternative methods (DCF, cost-of-capital benchmarking) supported by company-specific operating assumptions.
